Chose tokio over async-std for the async runtime. Tokio has wider ecosystem support, more crates depend on it, and the multi-threaded scheduler fits our workload better than async-std's work-stealing approach. Async-std had nicer API ergonomics but the ecosystem gap was decisive.

Picked tokio instead of async-std for async runtime. Tokio's ecosystem is broader, most async crates we need (hyper, sqlx, reqwest) depend on tokio. The multi-threaded scheduler also matches our workload. Async-std API was cleaner but ecosystem gap was too big.

Decided on tokio over async-std as async runtime. Reasons: tokio has wider ecosystem (most async libraries assume tokio), better multi-threaded scheduler for our workload pattern. Async-std lost despite nicer API because ecosystem support is the deciding factor.

Runtime decision: tokio over async-std. Ecosystem support is much wider on tokio (hyper, sqlx, reqwest, axum all assume tokio). Multi-threaded scheduler is also a better fit for our workload than async-std's. Cleaner API on async-std but ecosystem decided it.

Selected tokio rather than async-std for the async runtime. Tokio ecosystem is broader - most async crates we depend on (hyper, sqlx, reqwest) are tokio-based. Multi-threaded scheduler also fits our use case. Async-std had a nicer API but ecosystem gap was decisive.

SQLite WAL mode bug: concurrent writes from multiple connections hang when WAL file is on a network drive. Switched to journal_mode=DELETE for the test fixtures since they run on tmpfs. Production uses WAL on local disk which works fine. Root cause is SQLite's locking on networked filesystems, not our code.

Hit a SQLite WAL hang issue: concurrent writes hang when the WAL file lives on a network filesystem. Workaround: use journal_mode=DELETE for tests (they run on tmpfs anyway). Production runs WAL on local disk which is fine. Underlying issue is SQLite's behaviour on networked storage, not our code.

Debugged SQLite WAL bug today: concurrent writes hang when WAL is on a network drive. Fix: switched journal_mode to DELETE in test config since tests use tmpfs. Production keeps WAL on local disk and works correctly. Root cause is SQLite locking on network filesystems.

SQLite WAL concurrency bug: multiple connections writing concurrently hang if WAL is on a network filesystem. Resolved by setting journal_mode=DELETE for tests (which use tmpfs). Production uses WAL on local disk, no issue. The bug is in SQLite's network-filesystem locking, not ours.

Fixed SQLite WAL hang: concurrent writes hang when the WAL file is on networked storage. Solution: use journal_mode=DELETE for the test suite (tests run on tmpfs). Production continues using WAL on local disk where it works fine. Issue is SQLite's network-filesystem locking semantics.

Architecture: wrapping Lance's object_store layer with a SealedFileStoreProvider for at-rest encryption. The wrapper intercepts reads and writes, applies AEAD seal/unseal with per-file AAD, and Lance sees opaque ciphertext. This is cleaner than modifying Lance internals and survives Lance version upgrades. Key derivation via K3 KDF from the master key.

Design choice: SealedFileStoreProvider wraps Lance's object_store interface to add at-rest AEAD encryption. Read/write interception applies seal/unseal with per-file AAD; Lance sees only ciphertext. Cleaner than patching Lance and survives Lance upgrades. K3 KDF derives the at-rest key from the master key.

At-rest encryption architecture: SealedFileStoreProvider wraps the Lance object_store. The wrapper does AEAD seal/unseal with per-file AAD on every read/write so Lance only ever sees ciphertext. Better than modifying Lance directly because it survives version bumps. At-rest key derived via K3 KDF from the master key.

Chose to wrap Lance's object_store with a SealedFileStoreProvider for at-rest encryption rather than patching Lance internals. The wrapper applies AEAD seal/unseal with per-file AAD on read/write so Lance handles only opaque ciphertext. Approach survives Lance version upgrades. K3 KDF for the at-rest key from master.

Architecture decision for at-rest encryption: SealedFileStoreProvider as a wrapper around Lance's object_store interface. Read/write paths apply AEAD seal/unseal with per-file AAD; Lance only sees ciphertext. Cleaner than touching Lance internals and survives Lance upgrades. At-rest key via K3 KDF from master key.

Rust borrow checker rule: a value can have either one mutable reference OR any number of immutable references at the same time, never both. Lifetimes ensure references do not outlive what they point to. The compiler proves these properties statically so we get memory safety without garbage collection.

Rust borrow checker rule: a value can have either one mutable reference OR any number of immutable references at the same time, never both. Lifetimes ensure references do not outlive what they point to. The compiler enforces these properties statically so we get memory safety without garbage collection.

Started building the auth module in Claude Code, then switched to Cursor for the refactor pass because Cursor's multi-file edits handle the cross-cutting renames better. Final review back in Claude Code. Both tools have access to the same memory vault so context carries over seamlessly.

Auth module work: started in Claude Code, did the refactor in Cursor (better multi-file edits for cross-cutting renames), reviewed back in Claude Code. Memory vault shared between both tools so context transferred without re-priming either model.

Auth module development: drafted in Claude Code, refactored in Cursor (Cursor's multi-file edit is better for cross-cutting renames), final review back in Claude Code. Both share the same memory vault so the context flows between tools without re-explaining.

Worked on auth module across two tools: initial implementation in Claude Code, refactor pass in Cursor (better at multi-file cross-cutting renames), final review in Claude Code. Shared memory vault means no context loss switching tools.

Auth module: built initial version in Claude Code, switched to Cursor for the refactor (multi-file renames work better there), reviewed final version back in Claude Code. The shared memory vault carries context between tools so we didn't have to re-explain anything to either model.
